quotes.verbatim_quote: These are the formulas for the 3 basic chords and their extensions.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Frames the reference table as the canonical formula source.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The altered chords are not listed here but they mean just what they say. For instance, a C7b9b5
would be a cr chord with the 5th flatted and a>9th added — in other words the formula would be
1,3, 95, 97, 99.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explains that altered chord names compose mechanically from the symbol — student derives formulas without a lookup table.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The diminished and augmented chords do not really fit in any of the families but they are most like
the dominant chords in the way that they are used as you will see.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Classifies dim/aug functionally with dominants, guiding their use in progressions later in the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Remember that the 9, 11, and 13 tones may be thought of as the 2, 4, & 6 (and vice versa). So the
above formulas that have these tones may be written 2 ways. For example, a 7/6 chord could be
written as 1,3,5,57,13.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the dual-notation principle for compound vs simple intervals across the entire formula table.
